coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free pyrolysis synthesis of solid-state fluorescent carbon dots: Optical properties and latent fingerprint imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Kun
Li, Wanjun
Luo, Xiaoke
Kang, Xinhuang
Wen, Yanmei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention due to their remarkable optical properties and low cytotoxicity. However, most studies on CDs have predominantly focused on their fluorescence behavior in solution, because their π-π stacking interactions, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, and surface electron transitions can induce fluorescence quenching through aggregation. In this investigation, three variants of solid-state fluorescent CDs were synthesized using a solvent-free pyrolysis technique under atmospheric pressure at 200 °C. In the solvent-free CDs syntheses, urea as a dispersant, while the carbon precursors (m-phenylenediamine, DL-aspartic acid, and p-phenylenediamine) were diversified. The photoluminescence quantum yields of the resultant blue-, yellow-, and red-emitting CDs powders were measured to be 11.62, 3.59, and 0.57 %, respectively. Radiative transitions resulting from the eigenstates of CDs dominate. Fluorescence decay studies suggested that radiative transitions resulting from the eigenstates of the CDs dominate as the emission maximum shifts to the red region. These CDs exhibit outstanding solid-state fluorescence, enabling their successful utilization in latent fingerprinting imaging. Remarkably, the red-emitting CDs also exhibited blue room-temperature phosphorescence, observable for ~3 s.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-state fluorescent
Solvent-free
Pyrolysis
Multicolor

We would like to thank the researchers at the Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for their assistance with material characterization. This research was funded by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515010304 ), the Enhancing School with Innovation of Guangdong Ocean University (No. 230419054 ), and the Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Resource Utilization and Research (No. Q17092 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the preparation of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, and photographs showcasing their solid- and solution-phase fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp excitation. (B) Normalized photoluminescence spectra of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs powders.
(A–C) UV–visible spectra of the ethanolamine suspensions of the three CDs. (D–F) UV–visible spectra of the three CDs powders.

(A–C) Fluorescence excitation (in black) and emission (in red) spectra of the three CDs powders. (D–F) PL spectra of the three CDs powders, revealing distinct dominant emission peaks under different excitations. (G–I) PL decay curves and the corresponding fits for the three CDs powders. (J–L) Photostability of the three CDs powders under consistent maximum excitation for 1 h.

(A–C) 3D fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs ethanolamine solutions, respectively. (D–F) 3D fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs powders, respectively. (G–I) UCPL spectra of the three CDs ethanolamine solutions, respectively.
(A) Phosphorescence spectrum of R-CDs powder (λex = 365 nm). (B) Phosphorescence decay of the R-CDs powder. (C) Phosphorescence images of the R-CDs powder captured after the UV lamp was turned off.

(A–C) TEM images of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. Insets show the HR-TEM and FFT images. (D–F) Particle-size distribution curves of the three CDs. (G–I) XRD patterns, Raman spectra, and FT-IR spectra of the three CDs, respectively.
High-resolution C 1s (A–C), O 1s (D-F), and N 1s (G–I) XPS profiles of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively.
Fluorescence images of latent fingerprints on a glass substrate.

head.ce:title: Solvent-free pyrolysis synthesis of solid-state fluorescent carbon dots: Optical properties and latent fingerprint imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry and Environment Science, Guangdong Ocean University, Zhanjiang 524088, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry and Environment Science, Guangdong Ocean University, Zhanjiang 524088, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China University of Geosciences, Beijing 100083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, China University of Geosciences, Beijing, 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention due to their remarkable optical properties and low cytotoxicity. However, most studies on CDs have predominantly focused on their fluorescence behavior in solution, because their π-π stacking interactions, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, and surface electron transitions can induce fluorescence quenching through aggregation. In this investigation, three variants of solid-state fluorescent CDs were synthesized using a solvent-free pyrolysis technique under atmospheric pressure at 200 °C. In the solvent-free CDs syntheses, urea as a dispersant, while the carbon precursors (m-phenylenediamine, DL-aspartic acid, and p-phenylenediamine) were diversified. The photoluminescence quantum yields of the resultant blue-, yellow-, and red-emitting CDs powders were measured to be 11.62, 3.59, and 0.57 %, respectively. Radiative transitions resulting from the eigenstates of CDs dominate. Fluorescence decay studies suggested that radiative transitions resulting from the eigenstates of the CDs dominate as the emission maximum shifts to the red region. These CDs exhibit outstanding solid-state fluorescence, enabling their successful utilization in latent fingerprinting imaging. Remarkably, the red-emitting CDs also exhibited blue room-temperature phosphorescence, observable for ~3 s.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, comprising sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon nuclei with surface functional groups (size <10 nm) [1,2]. They are noteworthy for their stability and photobleaching resistance attributed to their sp2-conjugated structural domains [3], and are less toxicity than inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and thus cause minimal environmental harm. They showcase exceptional optical properties, tunable luminescence, favorable biocompatibility, and a distinctive electrical structure, which render them versatile for numerous applications including photocatalysis [4,5], bioimaging probes [6,7], photoelectric conversion [8,9], and optical anti-counterfeiting [10,11].

CDs commonly feature copious surface functional groups that function as both electron donors and acceptors. With increasing CD concentration in a solvent, the distance between the CDs diminishes, resulting in π-π stacking interactions, surface electron transition, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer between adjacent CDs. This close proximity of CDs triggers non-radiative transition modes. Moreover, their aggregation can result in fluorescence quenching, known as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [12,13]. The phenomenon of ACQ has limited the application of CDs in fields such as fluorescent probing, bioimaging, and sensing [14]. In response, solid-state fluorescent CDs (SSF-CDs) have emerged as a solution, combatting the ACQ issue that plagues conventional CDs in solution [15–17]. Thus, the pursuit of methodologies that facilitate the large-scale synthesis of SSF-CDs has gained momentum.

Currently, two primary methods are employed for preparing CDs: top-down and bottom-up approaches [18–20]. The top-down method involves the chemical or physical fragmentation of carbon-rich materials, followed by the processing of the products to restrict their size below 10 nm [21]. Conversely, the bottom-up method uses small organic molecules as precursors and employs pyrolysis or carbonization techniques to meticulously tailor the structure and properties of carbon materials at atomic and molecular scales, thereby realizing CDs [21]. Bottom-up techniques find wide application owing to their versatility in utilizing various carbon sources and the diversity of reaction conditions, which provide CDs with improved fluorescence properties [22,23].

However, bottom-up CDs synthesis often necessitates a high temperature, high pressure, and substantial quantities of a solvent, leading to hazardous conditions and intricate purification processes in closed reaction systems. These processes tend to be time-consuming and produce substantial amounts of waste and solvent residues [24–26]. Therefore, developing solvent-free methods for producing high-quality CDs is imperative to address these challenges effectively. In contrast to liquid-phase reactions, solvent-free reactions circumvent the need for solvents, aligning with the principles of green synthesis. Xiong et al. [27] reported the solvent-free synthesis of SSF-CDs that emit at ~620 nm by blending m-phenylene triol as the molecular precursor with boric acid as the dispersant, achieving up to 75 % yield. Similarly, Xu et al. [28] synthesized oil-soluble fluorescent CDs with a 551 nm emission peak and excellent solubility in diverse organic solvents using a solvent-free reaction of trianiline and 4-iodophenyldiphenylamine precursors. Furthermore, Zhang et al. [29] harnessed the dehydrative self‑carbonization effect of the SO3H group in the carbon precursor to fabricate solvent-free red-emitting CDs exhibiting emission in the wavelength range of 600 to 800 nm and a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 59 %. Similarly, Guo et al. [30] documented the production of green-emitting CDs with a high PLQY by heating a homogeneous precursor mixture of citric acid and cyanamide in an open container.

To address the issue of weak or non-existent luminescence of solid-state CDs due to ACQ, we drew inspiration from Wang et al. [31] and adopted a one-step solvent-free pyrolysis method. This approach is safe and convenient, and can be conducted at atmospheric pressure without complex post-processing procedures. We employed urea as the dispersant and m-phenylenediamine (m-PD), DL-aspartic acid, or p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) as the precursor for the efficient, green, and large-scale synthesis of three distinct types of SSF-CDs that emit blue (B-CDs, 420 nm), yellow (Y-CDs, 545 nm), or red (R-CDs, 630 nm) light, respectively. The produced CDs are soluble in most organic solvents, and the PLQYs of the synthesized CDs were measured to be 11.62 % (B-CDs), 3.59 % (Y-CDs), and 0.57 % (R-CDs). Our success in achieving CDs that emit light of different colors simply by varying the raw materials highlights the potential of this novel synthetic approach in future SSF-CD production. Remarkably, the R-CDs exhibited blue room-temperature phosphorescence. With their straightforward preparation and unique optical properties, these multicolor SSF-CDs hold promise for applications in LEDs, color screens, and fluorescence-based biological imaging.

The preparation of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs using m-PD, DL-aspartic acid, and p-PD, respectively, as carbon precursors is straightforward and was carried out under atmospheric pressure. Each of the carbon precursors was combined with urea in a threaded glass tube, heated at 200 °C for 30 min, and subsequently treated with water to induce the precipitation of the CDs. The resultant CDs were then collected by centrifugation, washed with water, and dried to obtain B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs as powders. Upon dispersion in solvents, these CDs demonstrated blue or green fluorescence (Fig. 1A). The normalized photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the three CD powders, when optimally excited, covered a broad spectrum ranging from blue to red (Fig. 1B).

The UV–visible spectra of the three CDs in ethanolamine (Fig. 2A–C) display similar absorption bands in the UV region (200–300 nm). The subtle absorption peak at ~325 nm indicates the presence of graphite (Fig. 2A) [32]. All CDs exhibit a single band in the range of 240 to 260 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic ring CC/CN bonds (Fig. 2A–C). Additionally, the UV–visible spectra of the three CDs powders (Fig. 2D–F) exhibit multiple UV–visible absorption bands spanning 200 to 600 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition of CN, the π-π* transition of CC, and the n-π* transition of CO within the carbon core [33–35].

Next, the photoluminescence (PL) performance of the CDs was investigated. The B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs exhibited maximum emission peaks at 420, 545, and 630 nm, respectively (Fig. 3A–C). The corresponding optimal excitation wavelengths are 360, 460, and 580 nm. At these optimal excitation wavelengths, the PLQY of the B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs powders were measured to be 11.62, 3.59, and 0.57 %, respectively (Fig. S1 and Table S1). These outcomes reveal that the blue, green, and red SSF-CDs cover a wide emission band spanning the visible light spectrum from 350 to 700 nm. This attribute renders them apt for constructing high color rendering index white LEDs. Remarkably, the PL spectra of the powders demonstrated an excitation-independent emission behavior across various excitation wavelengths. The solid-state fluorescence of the CDs seems intricately linked to their inherent structure. This inference is drawn from the consistent position of the characteristic emission peak, which remained unaltered, while the peak intensity varied with increasing excitation wavelength. This observation implies uniform surfaces for the three SSF-CDs, each hosting a single emission center [36–38].

To delve deeper into the fluorescence mechanism, we measured the PL lifetimes of the three CDs powders. The PL decay curves of all CDs demonstrated a bi-exponential trend, signifying similar fluorescence processes (Fig. 3G–I). The PL lifetime has a swift component (τ1) and a gradual component (τ2), and the contributions of τ1 and τ2 are given by B1 and B2. For CDs, τ1 represents the radiative recombination of eigenstates, and τ2 represents surface states [36]. As shown in Table S2, the average emission lifetime (τavg) of the CDs gradually decreased from 6.40 ns for B-CDs to 2.20 ns for R-CDs with the red shift in the emission maximum. The ratio of B1/B2 was used to estimate the interaction between the eigenstate and surface state. The B1/B2 ratio gradually increased with the red shift in fluorescence, indicating that the radiative transition generated by the eigenstate of the CDs became dominant [39,40].

Further, we evaluated the fluorescence stability of the CDs. Fig. 3J–L shows the fluorescence stability curves of the three CDs powders. Following 1 h of continuous irradiation with the optimal excitation light using a laser source, the Y-CDs exhibited only 3 % decay, and the R-CDs exhibited ~6 % decay but retained >94 % of their fluorescence intensity, signifying robust fluorescence stability. In contrast, the B-CDs exhibited a substantial decay of ~52 %, indicating poor fluorescence stability. Due to the high energy density of the excitation laser, the temperature of the B-CDs increased rapidly and exceeded the tolerance level under continuous laser irradiation, resulting in structural damage and decreased fluorescence stability. The thermogravimetric curve of the B-CDs sample shows that its thermal stability is significantly poorer than those of the Y-CDs and R-CDs (Fig. S5).

As illustrated in the right panel of Fig. 1A, the ethanolamine suspensions of the three CDs displayed blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation. To visually compare the solid- and solution-phase fluorescence properties of the three CDs samples, we recorded their 3D fluorescence spectra. Fig. 4A–C exhibits the 3D fluorescence spectra of the suspensions of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs in ethanolamine, each showing emission centers within the blue-to-green range. When superimposed with the 3D fluorescence spectra of the corresponding solid CDs (Fig. 4D–F), interesting findings emerged. The maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths of B-CDs were similar in both solution and solid states. Conversely, the solid-state Y-CDs and R-CDs exhibited considerably broader fluorescence emission wavelengths. Furthermore, the upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) spectra of the CDs suspensions were acquired with 800 nm light excitation. The suspensions exhibited blue UCPL, affirming structural similarities among the three CDs (Fig. 4G–I).

Phosphorescence is the emission of light owing to the transition from the excited triplet state to the ground state, which occurs for a few seconds after the light source is turned off. The R-CDs powder exhibited red fluorescence under a UV lamp, and upon turning off the light source, it showed blue-green phosphorescence at room temperature. The phosphorescence spectrum of the R-CDs powder with an emission maximum of 496 nm was obtained under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 5A). Further, the phosphorescence lifetime under 365 nm excitation consisted of three components, viz., 35.18 ms (5.13 %), 302.20 ms (30.33 %), and 912.28 ms (64.55 %), resulting in an average lifetime of 682 ms (Fig. 5B). The phosphorescence of the R-CDs powder could be observed for ~3 s with naked eyes (Fig. 5C). At 365 nm excitation, a slight additional blue emission was observed at 401 nm (Fig. S3), which is the reason for the shorter phosphorescence emission wavelength (496 nm) than the fluorescence emission wavelength (630 nm). This result indicates that the phosphorescence phenomenon is not related to the red fluorescence emission. This unique feature can promote its application in sensing, anti-counterfeiting tags, biological imaging, and LEDs.

The B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were further characterized to gain more insights into their structure and luminescence mechanism. The reaction was controlled and occurred uniformly under the solvent-free condition, which effectively prevented the agglomeration of the CDs. The morphology of the CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 6A–C, the three types of CDs displayed uniform particles of spherical shapes; they were well dispersed in the solvent and were free of agglomerates. The Fast Fourier transform (FFT) images in the insets exhibit a regular hexagonal pattern, indicating the presence of a graphene-like structure with conjugated carbons. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images revealed that B-CDs and R-CDs are well-defined single crystallites with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the spacing of the (100) crystallographic planes of graphene [41]. In contrast, Y-CDs exhibited a crystal plane spacing of 0.18 nm, corresponding to the (102) crystal planes of graphene [42]. As shown in Fig. 6D–F, the average particle sizes of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were determined to be 2.84, 2.60, and 2.96 nm, respectively (based on 50 CDs randomly selected from the TEM images). The particle size distribution of each CDs type exhibited broad variations with no apparent linear relationship, indicating that the particle size did not significantly affect their fluorescence properties. Thus, the quantum size effect is not responsible for the red shift in the fluorescence emission of the CDs.

Fig. 6G displays the XRD patterns of the CDs. The three CDs had highly crystalline structures with complex mixtures of crystallites. Notably, all CDs exhibited a sharp signature peak at 2θ = 28°, corresponding to the (110) crystal planes of graphitic carbon [43]. Additionally, the weak diffraction peak at 2θ = 12° corresponds to the (004) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [44]; these peaks indicate the graphitic structure of the CDs. Moreover, as the emission wavelength red-shifted, the intensities of the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 19° and 22°, corresponding to the (001) and (002) crystallographic planes of graphitic carbon, increased [45,46].

Raman spectroscopy was employed to further investigate the red-shift phenomenon of the emission peaks. Two broad Raman peaks appeared at 1400 and 1600 cm−1, corresponding to disordered sp3 (D band) and graphitized sp2 (G band) carbons, respectively, in the Raman spectra (Fig. 6H). These peaks indicate the graphitization degree of the CDs [47]. The relative intensity ratio of the D to G band (I
D /I
G ) was calculated to assess the degree of graphitization. Similar ID/IG values of 0.94, 0.95, and 0.94 were obtained for B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. This analysis reveals that the red shift in the fluorescence emission peaks of the CDs is not related to the degree of graphitization.

The chemical bonds and functional groups present in the CDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 6I reveal similar FTIR absorption patterns for the three CDs samples, suggesting comparable chemical structures. The spectra present absorption peaks corresponding to NH and CH stretching vibrations at approximately 3425 and 2810 cm−1, respectively [33,48]. The CO stretching vibration is observed at ~1720 cm−1 [49], and the peaks at ~1660, ~1600, and ~1415 cm−1 are attributed to aliphatic CC, aromatic ring CC, and CN bond stretching vibrations, respectively [33,50,51]. A peak of the COC stretching vibration also appeared at ~1360 cm−1 [52]. In the 900–1200 cm−1 range, several weak and sharp absorption peaks are observed, which correspond to the in-plane CH bond bending in aromatic rings [52].

XPS was performed to validate the FT-IR results and analyze the chemical bonding in the CDs. Fig. S2 and Table S3 show that the three CDs had similar elemental compositions (C, N, and O), indicating similar chemical bonding. The C 1s XPS profiles (Fig. 7A–C) reveal the presence of CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO bonds, representative of sp2, sp3, and oxidized carbons in the CDs [36]. Peaks corresponding to CO and COC bonds are also observed in the O 1s spectra (Fig. 7D–F) [53]. Finally, peaks corresponding to NH and CN bonds are observed in the N 1s spectra (Fig. 7G–I) [24]. Table S4 summarizes the quantitative data of the functional groups in these CDs, as extracted from the high-resolution XPS data. The NH bond content gradually decreased with the red-shift in the fluorescence emission of the CDs. The amino group content in the R-CDs was determined to be only 22.96 %. The reduction in the number of amino groups on the CDs surface is beneficial to obtain a narrow full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) [54]. This is consistent with our experimental results that the FWHM of the R-CDs is significantly smaller than those of B-CDs and Y-CDs. Conversely, the content of CN bonds increased from 36.13 % in B-CDs to 77.04 % in R-CDs, which may explain the differences in the PL properties among the three CDs types [55].

Fingerprints are reliable evidence in forensic medicine owing to their uniqueness and permanence. When a finger comes into contact with a solid surface, a fingerprint is left behind, and the fingerprint pattern can be used to accurately identify an individual. The synthesized CDs possess excellent solid-state luminescence properties, making them suitable for fingerprint imaging without interference from fluorescent backgrounds. When a finger, covered with sweat secreted from sweat pores, touches an object, the fingerprint residue remains on the surface. Such latent fingerprints are not easily visible to the naked eye but are commonly detected as evidence at crime scenes. The powder method is an effective technique for detecting latent fingerprints. The object is evenly coated with a powder that adheres to the fingerprint pattern. Excess powder is then carefully blown off, leaving only the powder on the ridges of the fingerprint pattern. We obtained a fingerprint on a glass substrate by pressing a finger and applied the ground CDs powder to the fingerprint pattern. As shown in Fig. 8 , when illuminated with 365 nm UV light, the shape, width, and edge contour of the fingerprint ridges became clearly visible. Additionally, finer details such as bifurcations, termination points, and minutiae within the fingerprint pattern could be easily observed. These details are crucial for accurate fingerprint recognition. These results demonstrate the potential of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs as promising candidates for latent fingerprint imaging.

To explore the versatility of this synthetic method, we conducted reactions under the same conditions but replaced the substrate with chloramine T, p-toluenesulfonamide, and o-phenylenediamine. This led to the synthesis of SSF-CDs emitting blue, green, and yellow light (Fig. S4). The successful synthesis of SSF-CDs using solvent-free pyrolysis demonstrates the versatility and potential of this method for efficient production.

In this study, we successfully synthesized blue, yellow, and red SSF-CDs with PLQY of 11.62, 3.59, and 0.57 %, respectively, using a solvent-free pyrolysis method at 200 °C. Our approach involved employing m-PD, DL-aspartic acid, and p-PD as carbon precursors, and urea as the dispersant. Comprehensive optical, compositional, and structural characterizations were carried out to understand the fluorescence mechanism of the CDs, and their potential for latent fingerprint imaging using the powder method was also demonstrated. Our analysis, encompassing performance assessment and meticulous morphological and structural investigations of the three SSF-CDs exhibiting different emission colors, unveiled intriguing observations. As the fluorescence emission red shifted, both the fluorescence quantum yield and lifetime decreased. With the red shift in fluorescence, the radiative transition generated by the eigenstate of the CDs became dominant. In particular, the red-emitting CDs displayed subtle blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation and unveiled a remarkable blue phosphorescence that was perceptible to the naked eye and persisted for approximately ~3 s.

Urea, m-phenylenediamine (m-PD), p-phenylenediamine (p-PD), DL-aspartic acid, ethanol, and ethanolamine were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., LTD, and Silong Science Co., Ltd., respectively. All reagents were of analytical purity and were used without further purification.

The reaction vessel was placed on a digital display heating magnetic stirrer (model RCT-D-S25) with an AHB-U09L-2 accessory. FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Thermo Scientific iN10 USA spectrophotometer. PL spectra were measured using an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrometer (TU-1901 and PerkinElmer Lambda950 USA). TEM images were obtained using a JEOL JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope. XPS analysis was conducted using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument. Fluorescence decay curves, phosphorescence, and quantum yield were obtained using an Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer UK. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Japanese Neo-Confucianism Smart Lab SE instrument. Raman spectroscopy was conducted using a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution instrument (Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 785 nm. Thermogravimetric analysis of the CDs was performed in the temperature range of 30 to 700 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere using a TA Discovery TGA 550 instrument (USA).

In a 15 mL threaded glass tube, 4 g of urea and 150 mg of m-PD were mixed and heated at 200 °C under atmospheric pressure for 30 min with stirring at 500 rpm. After the reaction, cold water was rapidly added, and the reaction mixture was stirred until the solid precipitated completely. The precipitate was then collected via centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was discarded. The precipitate was washed thrice with deionized water. Finally, the B-CD powder was obtained by drying at 60 °C. DL-Aspartic acid was used to prepare Y-CDs and p-PD was used to prepare R-CDs using the same procedure.

The absolute PLQY of the CD powder was determined using Edinburgh FLS 1000. The PLQY was calculated as
PLQY =
I emission
I absorb
where Iemission is the intensity of the PL of the CD sample, measured using an integrating sphere, and Iabsorb is the intensity of the light used to excite the CDs, measured using the integrating sphere.

Kun Luo: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Wanjun Li: Conceptualization. Xiaoke Luo: Conceptualization. Xinhuang Kang: Funding acquisition. Yanmei Wen: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors declare that only ChatGPT was used to check the language and no content was written. We would like to thank the researchers at the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their assistance with material characterization. This research was funded by the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515010304), the Enhancing School with Innovation of Guangdong Ocean University (No. 230419054), and the Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Resource Utilization and Research (No. Q17092).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.110837.

Environ. Chem. Lett.

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

Small

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of luminescent carbon dots with ultrahigh quantum yield and inherent folate receptor-positive cancer cell targetability
